nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs, rocky reef ledges, and overhangs. Tends to set up cleaning stations under ledges and inside large coral heads, often in association with resident moray eels and other reef fishes.

diet.notes: In the wild, feeds on ectoparasites and mucus picked from client fish at cleaning stations, supplemented with detritus and small invertebrates. In captivity has been reported to take frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, sinking pellets, and most prepared marine foods. Generally advised to offer small feedings several times per week and ensure the shrimp is not outcompeted by faster tankmates.

summary: The Atlantic counterpart to the Pacific Scarlet Skunk Cleaner Shrimp, with a yellow body, a continuous white dorsal stripe edged in red, and long white antennae used to flag down client fish. Generally considered a peaceful, reef-safe cleaner suited to most community reef tanks.
careNotes:
The Caribbean Cleaner Shrimp (Lysmata grabhami) is the tropical Atlantic counterpart to the more commonly traded Pacific Scarlet Skunk Cleaner (L. amboinensis). Husbandry is broadly similar, but this species has been reported to prefer slightly cooler temperatures and is somewhat less common in the trade.

In the wild it has been observed setting up cleaning stations under reef ledges and inside large coral heads across the Caribbean, Bahamas, Florida, Bermuda, the Gulf of Mexico, and the eastern Atlantic islands (Canary Islands, Madeira, Cape Verde). It is commonly reported to associate with resident moray eels and large grouper, picking ectoparasites and mucus from client fish that hold position at the station. In the aquarium it has been documented performing the same behavior on most fish that present themselves, waving its long white antennae and rocking from side to side to advertise.

Water parameters across sources are broadly consistent. Temperature is generally advised in the 22–26°C range; one source noted tolerance down to 18°C, but this is below typical reef-tank operating ranges. pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.023–1.025, and dKH 8–12 are the commonly cited targets. Like all marine invertebrates, this species is intolerant of copper-based medications and is sensitive to swings in salinity, temperature, and nitrate — drip acclimation over 45–60 minutes is generally advised.

Minimum tank size estimates vary widely: Reefapp cites ~50 L, Reeflex 160 L, and trade sources for the closely-related L. amboinensis typically cite around 76 L (20 US gal). 100 L has been adopted here as a cautious round value that gives a single shrimp or bonded pair enough rockwork for shelter and territory without demanding the larger Reeflex figure.

All Lysmata cleaner shrimp are simultaneous (protandric simultaneous) hermaphrodites — every adult functions as both male and female — so a pair is straightforward to obtain and they have been reported to spawn readily in captivity. Rearing larvae through the long planktonic phase remains difficult and commercial captive breeding has not become reliable enough to displace wild collection.

Reef-safety is widely reported as 'yes', though one source noted occasional irritation of corals when the shrimp climbs over them — this is generally not a problem. The shrimp will be predated by triggerfish, hawkfish, large predatory wrasses, lionfish, snappers, groupers and certain crabs, and these tankmates should be avoided.

Cross-source notes: adult size estimates span 2–6 cm (Reefguide 2–5 cm; Reefapp 5 cm; Reeflex 3–6 cm; Wikipedia up to 6 cm); 4–6 cm has been adopted reflecting the commonly attained adult size. Temperature spans 18–26°C (Reeflex wider range) to ~26°C (Reefapp single value) to 22–26°C (L. amboinensis comparison); 22–26°C has been adopted as the averaged hobby-realistic range. Care level was rated 'easy' (Reeflex), 'average' (Reefapp), and 'moderate' (KP Aquatics); 'intermediate' has been adopted reflecting the species' sensitivity to copper, swings in salinity, and the need for cautious acclimation.

Primary-source note: LiveAquaria does not currently maintain a dedicated product page for Lysmata grabhami (only the Pacific L. amboinensis). The LiveAquaria root was used as the designated primary; husbandry baseline was taken from LiveAquaria's L. amboinensis page and verified against the Caribbean-specific sources listed below.
breedingNotes: Lysmata cleaner shrimp are protandric simultaneous hermaphrodites — every adult functions as both male and female, so any two individuals can form a breeding pair. Spawning has been reported as relatively frequent in captivity, with the female-phase parent carrying clutches of green eggs under the pleopods between molts. Rearing the planktonic larvae through metamorphosis remains difficult and requires a dedicated larval-rearing setup (gentle circulation, continuous live phytoplankton and rotifer feeds, then nauplii). Captive-bred specimens reach the trade only sporadically.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria does not currently list a dedicated product page for Lysmata grabhami (Caribbean Cleaner Shrimp); only the Pacific Scarlet Skunk Cleaner (L. amboinensis) is in the catalog. The L. amboinensis page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/product/696/) was used as the husbandry baseline for the closely-related cousin (care level easy/moderate, max 10 cm, reef-safe yes, peaceful, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025, copper-sensitive). Caribbean-specific values were then cross-confirmed from the secondary sources listed below.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch; no dedicated wiki page for L. grabhami was reachable. Substituted Reefapp Encyclopedia as the working secondary.

additional.name: Reefapp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed species identity (Lysmata grabhami, Caribbean cleaner shrimp), adult size ~5 cm, native range Gulf of Mexico/West Atlantic, peaceful temperament, reef-safe, average care level, omnivorous diet (krill, mysis, artemia, detritus, parasites), and ~50 L minimum tank size with ~26°C target.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Lysmatidae), order (Decapoda), adult size 3–6 cm, depth 6–91 m, temperature 18–26°C, 160 L minimum tank, care level 'easy', hermaphroditic, and captive-breeding feasibility. Noted preference for cooler temperatures relative to L. amboinensis.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org (Caribbean Reefs)
additional.notes: Confirmed Caribbean/Bahamas/Florida native range, depth 1–27 m, size 2–5 cm, and trade-name synonyms (Red-Backed Cleaning Shrimp, Scarlet Lady).

additional.name: KP Aquatics
additional.notes: Florida-Keys collector. Confirmed Caribbean origin, ~5 cm max size, moderate care level, omnivorous diet, reef-safe, and incompatibility with triggerfish, groupers, large wrasses, and snappers.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally advised to expect molts roughly every 3–6 weeks in well-fed adults, slowing somewhat with age. The shed exoskeleton is typically left intact on the substrate and is sometimes consumed by the shrimp to recover minerals — leaving the molt in place for a day or two has been reported as common practice. Stable salinity and adequate iodine are generally advised to support clean molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn readily in the aquarium (the species is a protandric simultaneous hermaphrodite, so any two adults can pair). Rearing the planktonic larvae through metamorphosis remains the bottleneck and requires a dedicated larval setup with continuous live phytoplankton and rotifer feeds.
